coredata.dc:title: Experimental thermal runaway degradation and residual performance of fiber-reinforced PEEK and PPS composites for battery enclosure applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Etchegaray-Bello, Margarita
Millen, Scott L.J.
Fernández-de-Palencia-Navarro, María
Schöberl, Jan
Nguyen, The Anh Dieter
Körber, Leo
O’Connor, Evan
Schuhmann, Moritz
Drechsler, Klaus
coredata.dc:description: High-powered lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are adopted in many transport sectors to reduce carbon emissions. To ensure LIB safety, especially for electric vehicle (EV) applications, battery enclosures must be designed to withstand load cases and failure modes, such as thermal runaway (TR), while remaining lightweight to enhance vehicle range. Fiber-reinforced polymers (FRPs) represent a promising alternative to traditional materials like steel and aluminum for enclosures. This study investigates high-performance thermoplastic composites such as polyetheretherketone (PEEK) and polyphenylene sulfide (PPS), reinforced with glass (GF) and carbon fiber (CF) under multi-cell TR conditions. The samples are then subjected to a structural integrity assessment to quantify their damage. The testing method shows comparable open plies and residual compressive strength among samples from the same material. This supports its use for initial material screening prior to system-level evaluations. Structural integrity assessments show no clear correlation with the number of open plies and mass loss, emphasizing the need for subsequent mechanical testing methods. PPS samples showed increased resin degradation areas and lower residual compressive strength (CF-PPS retained 18.5 % while GF-PPS had 22.2 % residual compressive strength) compared to PEEK. GF reinforcement led to higher mass loss than CF and more open plies than CF-reinforced samples but showed higher residual compressive strength (20.7 and 23.2 % for CF-PEEK and GF-PEEK, respectively) and lower back-surface temperatures. The results herein can contribute to the assessment of suitable fiber–matrix systems under TR conditions for battery enclosure applications, by comparing failure mode and structural integrity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermal runaway
Battery enclosure
Thermoplastic
CFRP

Thermal behavior and fire response of FRPs
High-performance thermoplastic FRPs for battery enclosures
Combined loading compression of pristine laminates
Combined loading compression of pristine laminates
Residual compressive strength of damaged laminates

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported through the project BERTL within the Bavarian Aviation Research Program - Holistic Air Mobility Initiative granted by the Bavarian Ministry of Economic Affairs, Regional Development and Energy . (Grant number: HAM-2208-0031 ).

This research was supported through the project BERTL within the Bavarian Aviation Research Program - Holistic Air Mobility Initiative granted by the Bavarian Ministry of Economic Affairs, Regional Development and Energy. (Grant number: HAM-2208-0031). The authors thank Dr. Claudius Hamman from the TUM Fire Brigade for providing their facilities and expertise for the tests. The authors thank Ensinger GmbH for donating the specimens for the aim of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the test rig for the material screening of fiber-reinforced composites under thermal runaway conditions. 1) sample, 2) lid, 3) cells (initiator is represented with red), 4) cell holder, 5) thermal insulation, 6) pressure relief orifice (Ø15.25mm), 7) pressure transducer connection port, 8) cable port (left) [13] and section view of the test setup including the temperature (T) and pressure (P) measuring locations (right).
Modified CAI fixture for CaTR residual strength testing.
Frames from the multi-cell thermal runaway (TR) tests of the CF-PEEK-2 sample, showing the fume at the onset of the TR (left), the sparks (middle) and the jet fire during the TR (right).
Front-face photographs of the samples after multi-cell TR tests.
Various temperature profiles during the multi-cell thermal runaway test of CF-PEEK-1.
Temperature profile of the sample mid back face and average heating rate of the sample mid back face within the first 10 s of the test determined by linear regression.
Pressure profile during the multi-cell thermal runaway test of CF-PEEK-1 (left) and CF-PEEK-2 (right).
Exemplary pictures of the back and front side surface and 3D scan of the glass and carbon-reinforced PEEK and PPS samples.
Cross-sectional area of the samples’ mid-plane (front face downwards). Interface of the samples from the edge to the damage (left) and the center region (right).
Compressive stress of pristine laminates (left) and residual compressive stress of laminates subjected to multi-cell TR conditions (right).
Summary of the average conditions during the multi-cell thermal runaway screening of CF-PEEK, GF-PEEK, CF-PPS and GF-PPS laminates.
Average temperature of PEEK and PPS samples reinforced with carbon and glass fibers subjected to multi-cell thermal runaway.

Max. Temperature rear face laminate middle

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Standard deviation computation is not possible since values present the highest temperature registered before last measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Average pressure results for PEEK and PPS samples reinforced with carbon and glass fibers subjected to multi-cell thermal runaway.
Results of the average failure analysis for PEEK and PPS samples reinforced with carbon and glass fibers subjected to multi-cell thermal runaway.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The mass loss was registered after cleaning the samples subsequent to the TR tests to remove the cell residue.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The failure modes are defined as MD: matrix degradation, S: swelling, OP: open plies, R: rupture.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The failure mode only corresponds to the samples that contained the TR.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the compressive strength of carbon and glass reinforced PEEK and PPS samples.

head.ce:title: Experimental thermal runaway degradation and residual performance of fiber-reinforced PEEK and PPS composites for battery enclosure applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-powered lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are adopted in many transport sectors to reduce carbon emissions. To ensure LIB safety, especially for electric vehicle (EV) applications, battery enclosures must be designed to withstand load cases and failure modes, such as thermal runaway (TR), while remaining lightweight to enhance vehicle range. Fiber-reinforced polymers (FRPs) represent a promising alternative to traditional materials like steel and aluminum for enclosures. This study investigates high-performance thermoplastic composites such as polyetheretherketone (PEEK) and polyphenylene sulfide (PPS), reinforced with glass (GF) and carbon fiber (CF) under multi-cell TR conditions. The samples are then subjected to a structural integrity assessment to quantify their damage. The testing method shows comparable open plies and residual compressive strength among samples from the same material. This supports its use for initial material screening prior to system-level evaluations. Structural integrity assessments show no clear correlation with the number of open plies and mass loss, emphasizing the need for subsequent mechanical testing methods. PPS samples showed increased resin degradation areas and lower residual compressive strength (CF-PPS retained 18.5 % while GF-PPS had 22.2 % residual compressive strength) compared to PEEK. GF reinforcement led to higher mass loss than CF and more open plies than CF-reinforced samples but showed higher residual compressive strength (20.7 and 23.2 % for CF-PEEK and GF-PEEK, respectively) and lower back-surface temperatures. The results herein can contribute to the assessment of suitable fiber–matrix systems under TR conditions for battery enclosure applications, by comparing failure mode and structural integrity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of high-powered lithium-ion batteries (LIB) as a source of energy for aerospace, automotive or maritime sectors is experiencing growth, arising from the need to reduce carbon emissions across transportation. LIBs are one of the preferred choices for application in electric vehicles (EVs) due to their high specific energy, efficient energy storage capacity, and minimal self-discharge. However, their widespread adoption poses significant safety concerns due to their thermal sensitivity and the potential for failure under certain conditions outside normal safety and operational limits [1–4].

Thermal runaway (TR) is the ultimate failure mode of LIBs and consists of a rapid exothermic decomposition of the cell, resulting in the release of heat, high-temperature gases, and ejected material [5]. Diverse research has been carried out to study the TR of cells in various experimental setups under diverse abuse conditions (thermal [5–9], mechanical [5,6] and electrical [5]). These have studied the TR behavior, specifically heat release rate, released gas composition, flame height, mass loss, and temperature profile.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery enclosure materials and performance requirements
ce:section.ce:para: To mitigate the risks associated with cell TR, battery casings must protect the module from external impacts and contain the heat and gases released in the event of internal failure [10]. Screening potential materials at coupon level under severe conditions, such as a TR, is crucial but remains under-researched. The enclosure design for EVs introduces a dual challenge since it must be robust to ensure structural integrity for the load cases and possible failure modes [11], but its weight significantly impacts the vehicle's range. This reinforces the need for alternative lightweight casing materials such as fiber-reinforced polymers (FRP) over traditional metal counterparts like steel and aluminum [6]. Recently, the interest in FRP battery enclosures has been growing due to their low weight, impact resistance, thermal insulation, corrosion resistance, and design flexibility [11].

Battery fires exhibit distinct characteristics from conventional fire sources, such as high heat release rates, high localized peak temperatures, abrasive particle ejections, chemical reactions, high-pressure shock waves and short durations (often in the order of seconds) [10,11]. Understanding how potential materials perform under these extreme conditions is essential. However, limited research has been conducted on this type of thermo-mechanical testing.

Standard methodologies have been developed to assess materials under TR conditions. Nummy et al. [19] paved the way for the release of the UL2596 standard [12], which evaluates material behavior under conditions representative of a TR event. The standard involves the thermal trigger of two 18650 cylindrical cells, which propagate to their surrounding 23 cells. Schludi et al. [11] further adapted the UL2596 standard and investigated glass fiber (GF)-epoxy, with aluminum trihydroxides (ATH) flame retardant, and GF-phenolic systems, and found the phenolic system performed better in multi-cell TR, even with lower thickness than the epoxy.

In a previous study, the authors conducted preliminary screening of various matrices reinforced with carbon fibers (CF) to evaluate their suitability for battery enclosures [13]. Among the tested materials, polyetheretherketone (PEEK), polyphenylene sulfide (PPS), and an aerospace grade epoxy exhibited the lowest mass loss and minimal visual damage.

Höhne et al. [10] developed a bench-scale fuel fire test for energy storage system housing materials and studied the response of fiber-reinforced thermoset (TS) and thermoplastic (TP) sandwich structures. They provided several evaluation criteria, including structural integrity and mass loss, to quantify performance. Sterling et al. [14] recently developed a Finite Element (FE) model using literature data to investigate the thermal and mechanical damage for IM7/8552 composites during TR events. Additionally, they assessed the residual strength for the samples, using a so-called Compression after Thermal Runaway (CaTR) test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal behavior and fire response of FRPs
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to traditional structural components made from metals, e.g., steel and aluminum, composite materials are reactive under high temperature conditions due to their organic matrix, and if applicable organic fibers [15]. The thermal stability and fire properties of composite materials have been extensively characterized, using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) [16–21] and cone calorimetry [16,22,23] to determine their degradation onset, mass loss, ignition time, heat release rate, limiting oxygen index, flame spread, and smoke density.

Understanding the fire behavior of FRPs is challenging due to the complex interaction of thermal, chemical, physical, and failure mechanisms. Thermal processes include heat conduction from the fire and then through the composite, absorbed heat from matrix pyrolysis, organic fibers or organic core materials, convective heat loss, generated heat through the char or fiber oxidation, and released heat from ignition of flammable reaction gases [15]. Chemically, the matrix and fiber undergo decomposition, volatilization and char formation, growth and oxidation, and char-fiber reactions (depending on the nature of the composite) [15]. Physically, the fire exposure induces thermal expansion from thermally induced strains, internal pressure build-up due to formation of volatile gases and vaporisation of moisture, formation of gas-filled pores, matrix cracking, fiber–matrix debonding, delamination, surface ablation, softening, melting and fusion of fibers [15,24]. Failure processes vary depending on the thermal and mechanical loads, with thermoset composites exhibiting micro-buckling and thermoplastic composites plastic kink bands [15,25].

In general, composite material behaviour in fire is governed by the matrix. To a lesser extent, the fiber reinforcement has some influence on the thermal stability of the polymer [14,21]. The fibers, which have higher thermal stability as the resin, act as fillers, and thus, fiber-reinforced composites are generally less flammable than their pure resin counterparts [26]. Glass fibers (GF) are non-combustible and soften between 700–1000 °C, before melting [26,27]. In contrast, carbon fibers (CF) do not present thermoplastic behavior, but can fully decompose and/or oxidize under high-temperature conditions around 800 °C [26]. CFs typically possess a higher thermal conductivity than GFs [10,16,26], which results in more rapid heat transfer during thermal events. Furthermore, glass-reinforced composites exhibit a higher specific heat capacity [28,29], meaning they require more heat to increase their temperature by one degree celsius. These properties influence the thermal response of fiber-reinforced composites in high-temperature environments, such as TR. Studies using TGA [30] and cone calorimetry [20] show that CF composites ignite faster than GF composites, and GF-PEEK structures demonstrate better structural integrity under fire due to fiber fusion.

The fire response of FRPs has been widely studied [15,31–34] including research on protection layers for composites [35,36]. Nevertheless, experimental characterization and modelling efforts of composites have focused primarily on fire resistance, with limited studies addressing the structural performance of composites under fire conditions [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-performance thermoplastic FRPs for battery enclosures
ce:section.ce:para: Among the materials considered for battery enclosures, high-performance thermoplastic (TP) composites represent a promising alternative due to their high thermal resistance. Compared to traditional thermosetting matrices, such as epoxies and phenolics, high-performance thermoplastics, such as PEEK and PPS, have higher thermal stability [27]. TPs also offer various advantages for industrial applications, such as ease of manufacturing, outstanding damage tolerance, high impact resistance and good retention of their mechanical properties under fire conditions [27].

PEEK is one of the most thermally stable thermoplastics, with TGA data showing the onset of thermal degradation occurs at 520 or 555 °C under oxidative and inert environments, respectively [20]. Similarly, PPS exhibits degradation onset around 440 and 450 °C in oxidative and inert atmospheres, respectively [21].

Previous research has extensively studied the cell thermal runaway as a function of various factors [5–9]. Furthermore, the thermal stability and fire behavior of fiber-reinforced polymers has been investigated using TGA and cone calorimetry to determine fiber properties [20,26,37–39]. Advances in thermo-mechanical testing have emerged through the TR standard test and its adaptations, fuel fire test benches and FE modeling [10–14,40]. However, to date there is limited published data regarding lightweight battery enclosure materials, such as FRP, under TR conditions. Thus, this work aims to conduct an experimental multi-cell TR test to evaluate coupon-level FRP specimens, providing insights into their thermo-mechanical response prior to component- or vehicle-level resistance tests. These findings will contribute to the development of safer, lightweight enclosures for electric vehicles. Two high-performance thermoplastics (PEEK and PPS) were tested, with either CF or GF, and further analysed regarding their structural integrity by measuring their residual CaTR strength.

Four different semicrystalline thermoplastic composites were tested: CF-PEEK, GF-PEEK, CF-PPS and GF-PPS, described further in Table 1 . The samples consisted of 130mm × 130mm fiber-reinforced coupons made from weave fabric supplied by Ensinger GmbH. The CF laminates were reinforced with HT 3k fibers, while the GF samples were reinforced with E-glass. All samples contained a fiber volume fraction of 50 %. The samples were pre-conditioned at average temperature of 25.3 ± 2.05 °C and humidity of 50.8 ± 5.8 % for at least 24 h prior to TR testing. Each test condition was repeated three times, as specified by the UL 2596 standard [12], and due to the highly constrained nature of the test campaign because of the high cost and complexity of the setup. The mean thickness was calculated from five different measurements of each laminate. The thickness and weight of the samples are presented in the Supplementary data.
All tests were carried out with cylindrical 21700 li-ion cells with 100 % state of charge (SOC), as shown in Fig. 1 . The specification of the cells is provided in Table 7.

For validation of the cell capacity, the discharge capacities of 22 out of 84 of the tested cells were measured as a random sampling using a battery cycler (XCTS40, BaSyTec GmbH). The battery cells were fully charged with a 0.5C constant current (CC) charge followed by a constant voltage (CV) phase at 4.2 V up to a cutoff current of 100 mA according to the manufacturer's specifications. The discharge capacity was then determined with a C/3CC discharge up to the cutoff voltage of 2.5 V recommended by the manufacturer. The ambient temperature was kept constant at 25 °C during the measurement using a climatic chamber (KB400, Binder GmbH).

The TR test setup was based on the UL 2596 standard [12] with some adaptations regarding the number of cells (7), number of initiators (1), cell format (21700) and heating power (96 W). The resulting test apparatus is shown in Fig. 1 [13]. One initiator cell, placed in the middle of the surrounding six cells, was thermally triggered using a 96 W heating film (TC-9393192, Tru Components), executed using a microcontroller (Mega 2560, Arduino S.r.l.). The power was supplied to the heating films until TR onset (i.e., the abrupt change in the heating rate) was detected using a temperature feedback control at which point the microcontroller was turned off.

The test fixture was designed based on the UL 2596 standard [12] and was manufactured from structural steel. The diameter of the pressure relief orifice was 15.25 mm. A QuantumX data acquisition device (MX840B, Hottinger Brüel & Kjær) was used to record the temperature and pressure profile during the tests. The temperature was recorded using glass-fiber isolated K-type thermocouples (G/G-30-KK-IEC, Thermofühler GmbH) for the initiator cell (T3), two surrounding cells (T4), laminate back face (T1), and enclosure (T2), all shown in Fig. 1. The thermocouples were glued halfway up the cells. The temperature of the laminate was recorded using the thermocouples in the center and 20 mm away from the center (halfway between center and specimen holder). Additionally, the temperature of the sample was also recorded with an infrared camera (640i, Optris GmbH) throughout the test. The pressure inside the enclosure (P in Fig. 1) was registered using a differential pressure transducer (797–5046, RS Components GmbH).

The criteria for assessing the results was based on the work of Höhne et al. [10] regarding their criteria for mass loss and structural integrity. The mass loss of the specimens after the test provides information regarding the flame retardant behavior of the laminates, as it indicates how much material is lost by decomposition, dripping and detachment [10]. Höhne et al. [10] suggested that mass loss values in the range of 1–2 % for specimens with a thickness of 3 mm indicate that only the specimen surface is pyrolyzed during the test. The structural integrity of the samples consists of the materials ability to preserve its load-bearing capability, i.e., no presence of rupture, perforation, or failure of reinforcement fiber layers [10]. The presence of open plies is included in this criterion, like the work of Grange et al. [45]. After each TR test the specimen was removed from the fixture and the weight was registered. Since the weight of the samples was higher than the weight prior to TR testing (see samples in Fig. 4), the cell residues and char were removed manually from the samples using a brush and vacuum suction until the upper composite layer was uniformly exposed and visually free of contaminants. Care was taken not to damage the laminate surface. The weight was subsequently registered again to calculate the approximate mass loss. Fig. 8 shows representative samples after the cleaning procedure. Although this procedure cannot be reproduced with absolute precision, the same systematic approach was applied to all samples to ensure consistency and provide a qualitative indicator of material loss through degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combined loading compression of pristine laminates
ce:section.ce:para: The compressive strength of the pristine laminates was determined by Combined Loading Compression (CLC) following the ASTM D6641 standard [46], as recommended in ASTM D7137 standard [47]. Five samples were tested for each material using a universal testing machine (ProLine 100, ZwickRoell GmbH & Co) and one strain gauge on each face of the sample (MMF404140, Digi-Key Co.). The uniaxial compression test was performed with a displacement rate of 1.3 mm/min. The compressive strength of the pristine laminates was then used to calculate the residual strength of the damaged laminates.

For a better understanding of the damage tolerance properties of the studied materials, the residual compressive strength was studied for all the laminates subjected to the multi-cell thermal runaway testing using a modified Compression After Impact (CAI), CaTR test fixture (similar to the residual mechanical assessment of Vieille et al. [48]). The compressive load case was selected to allow testing of the full sample geometry without cutting the laminates, thereby avoiding variations associated with sample position within the laminate. The fixture suggested by the ASTM D7137 standard [47] was adapted to fit the samples (130x130 mm2), as shown in Fig. 2 . The uniaxial compression test was performed using the universal testing machine (ProLine 100, ZwickRoell GmbH & Co) on each laminate with a displacement rate of 1.25 mm/min.

The average determined discharge capacity of the battery cells is 5.009 ± 0.017Ah. Assuming deviations of up to 6-σ, over 99.99 % of the battery cells can be expected to be within a capacity range of 4.907 to 5.111Ah. Thus, the discharge capacities exceed the manufacturer's minimum requirement of 4.8Ah. No faults due to defective battery cells are, therefore, to be expected.

The multi-cell TR tests showed a violent reaction for all 12 tests (Fig. 3 ). The heating rate of the initiator cell using the 96 W power supply was 31.5 ± 6.3 °C/min (determined by linear fit using the test results). The time to reach TR was 198 ± 26s with an onset temperature of 131 ± 26 °C. The CF-PPS-3 sample, did not contain the TR of the initiator cell, and thus, the thermal propagation to the surrounding cells was not possible. This can be attributed to a defect in the sample or uneven stress distribution applied during the experimental setup. This sample was the only one considered invalid and was therefore excluded from the calculation of the average temperature and pressure conditions inside the enclosure. The maximum temperature inside the enclosure varied between 409 and 1350 °C for all the valid tests, with an average temperature of 930 ± 267 °C among all valid tests (i.e., those containing TR). The difference in the peak temperature and pressure conditions is attributed to the lack of repeatability between TR tests, where each uncontrolled exothermic reaction within the cell generates different energy and mass ejection rates. Nevertheless, the temperature of the samples, their mass loss, failure mode and their structural integrity are similar among the valid test repetitions.

After the tests, all the cells exhibited TR failure, however, the venting breakage of the cells was different among them, and frequently only the initiator cell vented facing the sample, and the rest vented in a radial direction. Fig. 4 presents an exemplar picture of the front face (the face towards the cell) of the samples after the cleaning for further weight measurement.

Fig. 5 shows exemplary temperature–time profiles for the initiator cell, the environment, two surrounding cells, and the laminate during a multi-cell thermal runaway test. The heating phase of the initiator cell through the heating film is observable from
t = 0
until it reached the TR condition and propagated to the surrounding cells approximately at 200 s. The temperature of the cells and the environment inside the enclosure reached temperatures above 800 °C. Comparing these values with the work of Nummy [49], where the 18650-initiator cell was triggered at a lower heating rate, both studies observed similar initiator cell peak temperatures of 1155 °C. However, the enclosure temperature herein was above 800 °C, compared to approximately 200 °C in ref. [49], despite fewer cells, but with a higher energy and trigger heating rate.

The temperature at the laminate middle appears to follow the expected profile. However, a sharp temperature drop occurs at around 330 °C, corresponding with the detachment of the thermocouple. The temperatures of the samples were then recorded using both thermocouples and an infrared camera (without the sudden drop, attributed to the debonding), as shown in Fig. 5. The results of the temperature profiles obtained with the data acquisition device were analysed for all the experiments. The average maximum temperatures within the enclosure and the maximum temperature of the samples are presented in Table 2 . Individual test data are available in the Supplementary data. The enclosure temperatures for the GF-PEEK-3 and the CF-PPS-1 were much lower than those recorded during the other tests. However, the sample temperatures were similar, which suggests the thermocouples may have malfunctioned during the tests.

Fig. 6 shows the temperature evolution of the samples’ middle during the TR exposure in the first 15 s after initiation. Despite the variability of each TR event, similar heating rates across samples and particularly within the same fiber–matrix combination were observed.

Within the same matrix family, Fig. 6 shows that the laminates reinforced with CF reach higher temperatures in the middle of the sample compared to the GF samples. This behavior was also observed by Höhne et al. [10] using polyester polyurethane resins and is attributed to the higher conductivity of the CF. Additionally, the GF has a higher specific heat capacity, and thus, would require additional heating to yield the same matrix degradation.

Fig. 6 presents the average heating rate of the samples at the beginning of TR, calculated using linear regression within the first 10 s after TR. For PPS, the temperature reached by the samples and the heating rate of the samples are higher when reinforced with CF compared to GF. However, for PEEK, there is no clear trend between the reinforcement fibers and the heating rate. A direct comparison between the temperature profiles of PEEK and PPS is not possible, as the materials differ in thickness by approximately 0.4 mm.

In Fig. 6, it can be seen that for CF-PPS-1 and CF-PPS-2 there is an inflection point when the material reaches around 225 °C, and a similar behaviour of GF-PPS-2 at around 235 °C. This might indicate a thermal transition within the material, such as melting, due to the high-temperature exposure. Although the datasheet melting point of PPS is around 285 °C (normally characterized at 10 °C/min), it is known that the onset of melting decreases with increasing heating rates [50,51]. Additionally, the plies near the back-face of the specimen, where higher temperatures are reached due to the thermal gradient, may be experiencing localized melting. Both effects could contribute to the observed inflection point, supporting the interpretation that this is associated with the melting of PPS.

In contrast to conventional fire sources, the rapid exothermic chain-reactions in battery fires cause sudden pressure increase [10]. Therefore, the pressure profiles during the multi-cell TR test were recorded for all tests. The maximum differential pressure lies between 0.94 and 3.43 bar, with an average of 1.99 ± 0.9 bar. These values are comparable with those found by Nummy [49], where a pressure relief orifice of 16 mm in diameter was used for a 25-cell setup. The summarized pressure results are presented in Table 3 . During the tests, the pressure profile inside the enclosure showed various peaks which were attributed to the TR pressure release of different cells. Each cell exhibited various peaks, making it difficult to attribute specific peaks to individual cells. The number and intensity of peaks varied across tests, which was expected due to the variability of TR events. Exemplar pressure profiles inside the enclosure for the CF-PEEK-1 and 2 samples are shown in Fig. 7 where the maximum pressure reached by both samples was above 1 bar. However, the CF-PEEK-1 sample experienced more peaks with higher intensity. The pressure peaks were analyzed for all the samples, using Origin with the “peak analysis tool”, where the basis line was selected as the mean and the method for finding peaks was chosen as the local maxima using a filter for peaks lower than 0.2 bar. Table 3 presents the results for all tests with the number of pressure peaks and the total area of the peaks found through integration, as well as the average peak height in bar and duration in seconds. The total area of the peaks, i.e., the time-integrated pressure, is a more reliable comparison between the tests, since it can represent the action density (in 1⋅105 J⋅s⋅m−3) throughout the test. The variance between the time-integrated pressure is very high, ranging from 0.61 to 5.89 bar⋅s among all valid (contained) tests, especially for CF-PPS and GF-PPS samples. Analysis of impulse and maximum pressure values in the enclosure revealed that they can be fit linearly proportional to one another; therefore, the maximum pressure will be used in the following sections of the discussion.

Fig. 8 shows the back and front (cell-facing) sides of four samples. All samples which contained TR show evidence of matrix degradation, swelling and open plies. The number of open plies is higher for samples reinforced with glass fibers. The matrix degradation on the back face of the samples is higher for the PPS samples, which can be attributed to lower thermal stability, i.e., lower onset of decomposition of the PPS matrix compared to the PEEK matrix.

After the tests, all the samples had char and cell residues on the front side. To quantify the damage of the samples, both non-destructive and destructive methods were used. First, the mass loss of each sample was retrieved after cleaning the cell residue and char. The second method measured the residual compressive strength of the samples after the TR event and will be presented in the Section 3.3.

Like the study of Grange et al. [45], where the fire resistance of carbon-reinforced polymers was assessed, the number of open plies was quantified in this study (see Fig. 8 right). To aid identification of open plies, the samples were scanned with a 3D measurement system (VR-5000, Keyence). The expansion of the sample at the surface was also observed by Grange et al. [45] during their cone calorimeter tests, and was attributed by various authors [27,45] to the accumulation of gas between the plies of the samples resulting from the resin degradation, and further transport of these gases to the surface of the composite material.

Despite the variability of the TR tests, the results shown in Table 4 regarding the non-destructive quantifiable damage, such as open plies and mass loss, are comparable among the valid tests. The mass loss of the samples was 2.6 ± 1.6 % for CF-PPS, 3.4 ± 0.2 % for CF-PEEK, 4.6 ± 0.4 % for GF-PEEK and, 7.0 ± 1.6 % for GF-PPS. In general, the samples reinforced with GF presented higher mass loss compared to their corresponding CF counterparts. This behaviour was also observed by Höhne et al. [10] using a fuel test fire. Furthermore, the variability of the mass loss values for the PPS samples was higher than the PEEK samples. This can be attributed to the lower thermal stability of the sample and the high variability of the explosions. The PPS samples have a higher degradation due to its lower thermal onset, and thus, it is more difficult to reproduce the results.

As discussed previously, the onset of thermal degradation was lower for CF-PEEK compared to GF-PEEK [20]. Conversely, in this study, the GF-PEEK samples exhibited higher mass loss than the CF-PEEK samples. This observation is attributed to the combination of the oxidative (upper layers) and inert (lower layers) environment within the samples in this study.

After completing the mechanical characterization, the laminates were cut in half using a diamond saw, and the cross-sectional area was observed through the thickness direction (cf. Fig. 9 ). The edges of all samples were clamped during the test (as shown in Fig. 1), and thus, show no damage. The edge and the center were further analyzed under the microscope.

Schuhler et al. [52] investigated CF-PPS specimens under unidirectional heat flux and observed degradation mechanisms consistent with the ones identified in the present study. Upon exposure to one-sided heat flux, the composite develops a through-thickness thermal gradient at the beginning of the exposure, generating thermally-induced strains that cause matrix cracking and interlaminar debonding. As the temperature increases through the thickness, these structural discontinuities act as thermal barriers, altering the effective thermal conductivity across these affected regions. The pyrolysis gas generation (from the matrix decomposition) within these delaminated zones creates internal pressure, resulting in macroscopic swelling of the composite material.

Microscopic analysis reveals distinct failure patterns across the material systems. CF-PEEK exhibited a single open ply at the surface and delamination in the subsequent four plies along the TR-affected zone, primarily due to matrix degradation. In contrast, CF-PPS demonstrated more extensive delamination and complete layer separation, with degradation propagating through the entire thickness along the exposed length since the back face temperature reached temperatures above the onset of matrix decomposition for CF-PPS (see Fig. 6). The glass fiber reinforced variants (GF-PEEK and GF-PPS) displayed more pronounced swelling. The plies closer to the back face remained comparatively less affected than those in the CF-PPS samples. The delamination zone for the GF-reinforced samples is mostly concentrated near the TR-exposed face, which may be attributed to the lower thermal conductivity of the glass fibers (0.37 W/m-K in the through-thickness direction for GF-epoxy [53]) compared to that of the carbon fibers (1.23 W/m-K in the through-thickness direction for CF-epoxy [53]). This difference results in a higher thermal through-thickness gradient, creating an effective thermal insulation barrier that protects the posterior plies from severe thermal degradation.
To further assess the structural integrity of the samples in a quantitative way, the analysis of the residual strength is presented in this section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combined loading compression of pristine laminates
ce:section.ce:para: The compressive strength results for the pristine laminates are shown in Fig. 10 (left) and the average compressive strength of the pristine samples is presented in Table 5 . All samples showed acceptable CLC failure modes classified in the standard (ASTM D6641 [46]). CF-PPS, GF-PEEK and GF-PPS failed in the through-thickness at grip top (HAT) mode, while CF-PEEK samples present the brooming gauge middle (BGM) failure mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Residual compressive strength of damaged laminates
ce:section.ce:para: The residual compressive behavior and strength of the laminates is shown in Fig. 10 (right). The failure mode during the CaTR test is valid among all samples, as classified in the standard ASTM D7137 [47]. All samples exhibited failure in the lateral through damage middle (LDM) mode with the exception of GF-PEEK-1 and GF-PPS-1, which failed in the lateral through damage right (LDR) mode. The average residual compressive strength is presented in Table 5.

Although the test method presents limitations regarding the reproducibility of the cell fire, especially for the pressure profile, the results of the failure analysis of the samples, both destructive and non-destructive show that the samples experienced a similar impact from the multi-cell TR. This resulted in comparable levels of open plies and residual compressive strength among samples of the same material even though there was a large variance in the temperatures and pressures measured within the enclosure. Thus, the method may be potentially used to screen candidate materials prior to system-level evaluations.

Although qualitative trends between temperature, pressure, and residual strength could be observed, the limited number of tests prevents from establishing statistically significant correlations. Therefore, these relationships are discussed in qualitative terms only. Fig. 11 presents the summary of the sample conditions during the test, mass loss, and residual compressive strength. In general, the CF-reinforced samples exhibited fewer open plies and a lower percentage of mass loss, indicating the higher resistance of the fibers against thermal runaway. However, when comparing these nondestructive observations with the structural integrity of the samples, no clear trend was identified between the number of open plies, mass loss, and the residual compressive strength of the materials. This can be explained through the delamination and through-thickness matrix degradation, and thus layer separation in the through-thickness direction. This effect is more prominent for CF-PPS, which present on average one open ply but shows extensive delamination through-thickness. Additionally, various factors such as char fraction within the sample, and structural integrity of the char can further impact the residual strength. This highlights the importance of a mechanical assessment, such as CaTR, as demonstrated in this study.

No consistent correlation was observed between pressure and residual strength, possibly due to the relatively low pressure levels and short duration of the peaks (see Table 1). It is therefore suggested that temperature played the dominant role in influencing the structural integrity of the samples, as polymer degradation and localized burning of the matrix were observed in the visual inspection of all samples that contained the TR. Furthermore, when comparing the temperatures reached at the back face of the laminates, phase transitions of the matrix, such as melting, were identified in the temperature profiles. These samples corresponded to lower residual compressive strengths. While this may suggest that pressure could contribute indirectly, for example through the enthalpy of the hot gases, the thermocouple data mainly captures the thermal effects, which likely include any such contributions. It must be noted however that the ablation resulting from the cell is not quantified in this study.

The materials tested in this work, except for one sample, were able to contain the TR with less than 10 % mass loss, indicating that, within the limitations of this study, sample thickness, and thus weight, could potentially be optimized. However, after TR, approximately one quarter of the initial compressive strength was conserved. GF-PEEK exhibited higher residual compressive strength with 23.2 %, followed by GF-PPS with 22.2 %, CF-PEEK with 20.7 % and lastly, CF-PPS with 18.5 %. Due to variations in thickness, it is challenging to directly compare the matrix materials, though some comparison is possible in terms of their thermal properties. The front-face damage of the PPS samples was greater than the PEEK samples, likely due to the lower thermal stability and fire resistance of PPS.

The difference in burning behavior between polymers reinforced with GF or CF is highly dependent on the burning test carried out [20]. For the multi-cell TR in this study, among samples with the same matrix, those reinforced with glass fibers exhibited higher mass loss and a greater number of open plies. However, GF-reinforced samples maintained lower back-surface temperatures compared to their CF counterparts, which may be related to the different thermal conductivities and inertia of the fibers, an effect which is increased for the PPS samples.

Thermal runaway (TR) is a complex phenomenon involving a combination of thermal and mechanical effects on enclosure materials, resulting in phase transitions, thermal degradation, delamination, and potential rupture of the structure. Due to limited existing literature regarding the effect of battery fires on composite materials it is challenging to understand these effects in a way which could inform future lightweight enclosures. In this work high-performance thermoplastic composites, (polyetheretherketone (PEEK) and polyphenylene sulfide (PPS)), each reinforced with glass (GF) or carbon fiber (CF) were tested under multi-cell TR conditions before completing both non-destructive and destructive structural integrity assessment to determine the extent of damage and residual compressive strength. Despite the variability of multi-cell TR, the testing method shows comparable open plies and residual compressive strength among samples from the same material. This supports its use for initial material screening prior to system-level evaluations. The CaTR tests show no clear trend with the results of the non-destructive testing failure analysis (open plies and mass loss), which emphasizes the need for subsequent mechanical characterization methods to quantify the results.

Mechanical compressive characterization reveals significant strength losses, with GF-PEEK retaining 23.2 % of its initial compressive strength, followed by GF-PPS (22.2 %), CF-PEEK (20.7 %), and CF-PPS (18.5 %). PPS-based samples exhibit larger resin degradation areas and lower residual compressive strength compared to PEEK and showed the only uncontained failure. GF reinforcement led to higher mass loss and more open plies than CF-reinforced samples but showed higher percentual residual compressive strength and lower back-surface temperatures.

While the results herein are not sufficient for a statistical analysis or optimisation of materials for battery enclosures based on temperature and pressure exerted on the enclosure, they can act as a useful framework for the assessment of candidate materials. The use of other characterization techniques, such as TGA, various fire tests and further FE modelling can aid the understanding and optimization of future lightweight composite-based battery enclosures.

Margarita Etchegaray-Bello: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Scott L.J. Millen: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. María Fernández-de-Palencia-Navarro: Software, Investigation. Jan Schöberl: Methodology, Investigation. The Anh Dieter Nguyen: Methodology, Investigation. Leo Körber: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Evan O’Connor: Methodology, Investigation. Moritz Schuhmann: Methodology, Investigation. Klaus Drechsler: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported through the project BERTL within the Bavarian Aviation Research Program - Holistic Air Mobility Initiative granted by the Bavarian Ministry of Economic Affairs, Regional Development and Energy. (Grant number: HAM-2208-0031).

The authors thank Dr. Claudius Hamman from the TUM Fire Brigade for providing their facilities and expertise for the tests. The authors thank Ensinger GmbH for donating the specimens for the aim of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.engfailanal.2025.110430.

ce:section.ce:para:
